items.title: Polar processes set Arctic marine heatwaves apart
items.body:
Download PDF

Perspective
    
        
            Open access

Published: 06 June 2026

Polar processes set Arctic marine heatwaves apart
                    Marylou Athanase 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0001-6603-98701, Ruijian Gou2 na1, Eike E. Köhn 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0003-2988-66883 na1, Benjamin Richaud4 na1 & …Amélie Simon 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0003-0177-94425 na1 Show authors

Communications Earth & Environment

volume 7,  Article number: 485 (2026) Cite this article

Subjects
        
            Climate changeMarine biologyMarine chemistryPhysical oceanography

AbstractThe Arctic is warming faster than any other ocean, yet its marine heatwaves remain poorly understood. No comprehensive assessment exists, particularly below the surface. Here we synthesise published Arctic marine heatwave studies alongside broader oceanographic literature, and offer our perspective on their drivers, impacts, and methodological challenges. Arctic marginal seas emerge consistently as surface marine heatwave hotspots, while subsurface events remain almost entirely uncharacterised despite likely being as intense and longer-lasting. Our synthesis reveals that Arctic marine heatwaves are shaped by processes absent from lower-latitude oceans: sea ice modulates heat fluxes, stratification, and temperature variability, while upward fluxes of warm subsurface water represent an overlooked driver class. We further identify key unknowns, including impacts on primary production and carbon exchange. Finally, we provide concrete methodological recommendations and priority directions for Arctic marine heatwave research: advancing this field is essential for anticipating ecological and climatic responses, in the Arctic and beyond.

IntroductionUnder accelerating global ocean warming, marine heatwaves (MHWs) are intensifying worldwide, becoming longer, larger, and more frequent1,2,3. MHWs are prolonged periods of anomalously high ocean temperature4. They arise from local atmospheric forcing or oceanic processes, including advection, mixing, and stratification changes5,6—with reduced winds and shallow mixed layers typically amplifying their surface expression. MHWs have profound impacts7, affecting ecosystems8,9, disrupting fisheries10,11, and altering biogeochemical cycles12,13. Beyond the surface, subsurface MHWs are increasingly reported14,15,16, threatening deep ecosystems17,18.Although most MHW research has focused on mid- and low-latitude oceans, recent studies show that Arctic MHWs have increased markedly in recent decades, with some of the largest observed trends globally in frequency, duration, and intensity19,20. Arctic MHWs can be more intense than their lower-latitude counterparts, with sea surface temperature (SST) anomalies reaching ~4 °C20,21. Notable events include the 2018–2019 Chukchi-Bering Sea MHW that led to a collapse of the snow crab population11, and the exceptional 2020 eastern Arctic MHWs22. These trends are linked to Arctic Amplification, whereby Arctic surface warming reaches two to four times the global mean23,24 with associated rapid sea-ice decline25. Both are projected to continue, driving future MHW increases that are expected to remain among the largest globally2,26,27.Understanding MHWs in the Arctic requires considering its distinct oceanographic characteristics. A defining feature is the extensive sea-ice cover limiting direct ocean–atmosphere heat exchange. Unlike non-polar oceans, where the warmest waters are near the surface, Arctic stratification is primarily controlled by salinity. Fresh water inputs from rivers, sea-ice melt, and glacier discharge create a cold and fresh surface layer separated from deeper waters by a strong halocline28. Beneath lies warm, salty Atlantic Water entering through the Fram Strait and Barents Sea29,30, whose northward reach is progressively expanding through Atlantification31. On the Canadian side, Pacific-origin waters form an additional shallow warm layer overlying the Atlantic Water30,32. Both provide a key subsurface heat source potentially influencing MHW intensity and vertical structure.Arctic MHWs have cascading impacts on local ecosystems and biogeochemical cycles33,34. Arctic primary production is strongly controlled by light, which depends on sea-ice cover and the polar day–night cycle, and by nutrient availability35,36,37. The latter is influenced by inputs from rivers38 and Atlantic and Pacific inflows, though strong salinity-driven stratification often limits vertical mixing and nutrient supply39. The Arctic exhibits relatively low biodiversity17,40, with cold-adapted species of narrow thermal tolerances34,41, making ecosystems particularly sensitive to MHW conditions. The Arctic is also a moderate but increasing contributor to global CO2 uptake42,43 and is acidifying about three times faster than other basins44, which adds to the increasing MHW-induced stress.Despite these distinctive conditions, dedicated Arctic MHW studies remain scarce, focusing predominantly on surface statistics in marginal seas19,20,26,27,33,34,45. Broader oceanographic literature describing MHW-relevant processes exists, but has not been synthesized through a MHW framing. There is consequently no comprehensive assessment of Arctic MHW properties, drivers, impacts, or their interplay—especially below the surface. Applying MHW definitions to the Arctic also raises methodological questions, particularly regarding baseline choices and metric adequacy against a rapidly changing background climate26,46.This Perspective synthesizes available Arctic MHW studies alongside broader Arctic oceanographic literature to address: What are the characteristics, drivers, and impacts of Arctic MHWs at the sea surface and below? Throughout, we distinguish established mechanisms from plausible but undocumented ones, and outline remaining unknowns. We provide an overview of MHW statistics aggregated across studies (Fig. 1a; Supplementary Table 1). We then offer our perspective on Arctic-specific drivers and impacts, proposing in particular that processes tapping into the subsurface heat complement the documented role of sea ice-ocean feedbacks in driving Arctic MHWs. Several unknown effects are identified: the net influence of Arctic clouds and ocean eddies on MHWs, and impacts of Arctic MHWs on air–sea CO₂ fluxes and primary production. Finally, we critically assess methodological challenges specific to the Arctic and propose concrete recommendations for tailoring MHW methods to this region.Fig. 1: Systematic literature assessment of Arctic MHWs.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imagea Studies examining surface and/or subsurface MHWs in seven Arctic sectors. Pie chart size indicates the relative number of studies. Sectors are adapted from Richaud et al.153, north of 60°N. Bathymetry (GEBCO Compilation Group154) is in background color scale; white is shallow, dark is deep. b Data type used to derive MHW statistics. c Detection definition. H16: Hobday et al.4 definition, with or without the following adjusted criteria: dur10d: minimum 10-day duration; gap3d: 3-day gap tolerance; 95th: 95th percentile threshold; LMST: SST exceeding the long-term mean summer temperature. d Framing of MHW driver analyses: we differentiate between studies examining mechanisms (local drivers of individual events) and attribution (MHW trends from climate change). Source data are provided in Supplementary Table 1.Defining marine heatwaves in the ArcticWe identify Arctic MHW studies using the set of search criteria detailed in the Methods. Arctic MHWs have been characterized primarily using daily data from satellite products and numerical model simulations (Fig. 1b), given sparse in-situ measurements and large biases of ocean reanalyses in ice-covered regions47,48. MHWs are defined as discrete events during which ocean temperatures exceed a seasonally varying threshold, most commonly the 90th percentile following Hobday et al.4. This definition derives the threshold against a 30-year baseline period with an 11-day moving window and subsequent smoothing, and conventionally requires a minimum MHW duration of 5 days with a 2-day gap tolerance. While its consistent application enables comparability of MHW statistics (Fig. 1c), it also raises several methodological questions specific to Arctic conditions.Detection criteriaThe commonly used 90th percentile threshold does not correspond to equivalent biological stress across ecosystems. In sea ice-covered regions, SST variability is strongly dampened, so a 90th percentile exceedance can represent only a fraction of a degree of warming, yet still impact near-freezing adapted organisms. Conversely, some studies adopt a 95th percentile combined with SST exceeding the long-term mean summer temperature49 to target more extreme heat stress and reduce artefacts from uncertain Arctic SST data20,22,50. The definitions are each well justified, but they reflect different biological implications, and the underlying biological question should guide percentile choice.Few studies have deviated from the standard 5-day duration and 2-day gap criteria (Fig. 1c), yet their biological relevance is species- and life-stage-specific. Longer minimum durations have been proposed for species sensitive to sustained warming51, while gap structure has been shown to influence Arctic phytoplankton productivity52.MetricsMost Arctic MHW studies report mean intensity (average SST anomaly over the event), while mean duration and frequency are less consistently provided: some instead report the total MHW days per year21,27,45. Maximum intensity is seldom included20,21,53,54, yet it is particularly relevant in the Arctic, as it indicates whether thermal thresholds important for cold-adapted species are exceeded. Cumulative intensity is sometimes used as an integrated measure of event magnitude19,33,55 and should be applied more consistently. However, it should complement rather than replace other metrics, as it cannot distinguish between short, intense events and longer, moderate ones with differing ecological impacts. Few studies51,56 report MHW categories57: in the Arctic, this classification is complicated by rapid ice-driven changes in SST variance (i.e., the statistical spread of temperatures around the mean), which can inflate severity categorization.Spatially, Arctic MHWs have been detected independently at each grid cell, as is common practice globally, meaning that areal extent remains undocumented and coherent warm water masses can be artificially fragmented. The MHW coverage ratio, defined as the fraction of the Arctic Ocean north of 60°N experiencing MHW conditions at a given time, offers one alternative20.BaselinesAn important methodological consideration when detecting MHWs is the choice of the baseline from which to compute the threshold. This choice should be guided by research objectives, ecosystem adaptation timescales, and whether separating long-term warming from variance changes is needed46.The fixed baseline, the most widely used approach in Arctic MHW studies19,20,22,33,45,50,51,53,54,55,58, is usually computed over a 30-year reference period spanning typically the 1980s to the 2010s or 2020s. It does not account for long-term changes in the mean. As Arctic warming continues, large portions of the Arctic Ocean will eventually be detected as being in a state of near-perennial MHW, at which point the fixed baseline risks losing physical meaning (Supplementary Fig. 1). However, it remains necessary for quantifying the contribution of long-term warming to MHW statistics, and for assessing impacts on slow-adapting Arctic ecosystems. The fixed baseline also does not account for long-term changes in variance at any timescale (Supplementary Fig. 2a).Indeed, sea ice presence affects not only warming trends but also SST variance (Supplementary Note 1, Supplementary Fig. 2). In the Arctic Ocean, warming trends are seasonally heterogeneous, with summer temperatures rising faster than winter ice-constrained temperatures59. Under-ice temperatures vary little, governed by salinity-driven melt-freeze processes and oceanic advection or mixing, while open-water temperatures respond rapidly to atmospheric forcing, implying different variance timescales60. The transition from perennial to seasonal ice cover thus produces long-term changes in SST variance across three timescales: interannual (year-to-year variability), seasonal (amplitude of the seasonal cycle), and subseasonal (days-to-months shift from slowly evolving under-ice temperatures to atmospherically driven fluctuations when sea-ice retreats). Each of these variance timescales can exhibit anthropogenic long-term changes, and whether they need to be isolated depends on the research question.Alternative baseline approaches differ in which of these variance changes they account for, a distinction particularly consequential in the Arctic compared to lower latitudes. The typical detrended baseline46, where the same detrending is applied to all months21,26,56, removes long-term mean warming only but ignores trend seasonality and variance changes at any timescale, and is thus not well suited for Arctic applications (Supplementary Figs. 1 and 2b). A daily-varying trend calculation61 additionally accounts for variance changes at seasonal timescales and partially at subseasonal timescales. The shifting baseline46 accounts for changes in mean and variance across all timescales, and is thus better suited to detect Arctic MHWs beyond those explained by long-term changes alone27 (Supplementary Figs. 1 and 2c).Characteristics of Arctic marine heatwavesArctic MHWs are among the most intense reported globally: maximum SST anomalies can exceed 4 °C20,21,22, which is comparable to or surpasses those recorded in lower-latitude oceans. We compiled all studies providing surface MHW metrics and aggregated them into cross-study ranges for seven oceanographically distinct Arctic sectors (Fig. 2). We restrict this aggregation to fixed-baseline metrics, as it is the only approach consistently applied across all reviewed studies. Coverage is, however, uneven, with the Barents-Kara Seas being the most documented and the Central Arctic the least (Fig. 1a). As Arctic MHW seasonality remains unstudied, all metrics reported here reflect yearly averages. Only two studies examine subsurface conditions51,54: their findings are reviewed below but not aggregated.Fig. 2: Surface Arctic MHW characteristics in the assessed literature.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imagea–g Mean intensity (°C), and h–n mean duration (days) per Arctic sector. The study index (1 to 15) is in x-axis (Supplementary Table 1). The study using a 10-day minimum duration is marked with a star in (h–n). For each panel, the range of minimum to maximum values for each study are in gray vertical lines. The mean range (mean of minima to mean of maxima) is in gray shades. The median and mean of mid-points are in plain colored and dashed black horizontal lines, respectively. The interquartile range of mid-points (IQR; Q1 to Q3) is in colored shades. In (j), Golubeva et al.21 reports durations up to 300 days; the y-axis is limited to 100 days.Regional propertiesMean intensity is highest in the marginal seas, particularly the Siberian, Barents-Kara, and Beaufort Seas, ranging from about 1 to 3 °C (Fig. 2a–g). Severe events are predominantly concentrated over shelves and along first-year ice edges19,20,22,27. The reasons for this are discussed in the following section. In contrast, mean intensity is lowest in the Central Arctic. Duration is comparatively homogeneous across sectors, ranging approximately from 10 to 40 days, with the longest durations reported in the Barents-Kara and Siberian Seas (Fig. 2h–n). Frequency is lower than or similar to other ocean basins and is less consistently reported, with estimates ranging from 1 to 3 events per year in all Arctic sectors (Supplementary Fig. 3). The Beaufort Sea emerges as an important but understudied hotspot, with mean intensity comparable to other marginal seas yet no dedicated regional study (Fig. 2e).The few available subsurface studies suggest that Arctic subsurface MHWs present distinct characteristics from their surface counterparts. Using a fixed baseline, they can reach similar intensities as surface events, but persist considerably longer, from several months to years17,54. Regionally, subsurface MHWs have been detected as deep as 500 m near the western Barents Sea shelf51 and at the ocean floor in the Barents Sea54.Most prominent events were observed in the Barents-Kara and Siberian Seas. The 2016 MHW in the Barents Sea45,54 was the longest on record (480 days), with similar intensities detected at both the surface (1.3 °C) and ocean floor (1.1 °C). The 2020 Barents-Kara and Siberian Seas surface MHW was the most severe so far, with a mean intensity of 4 °C and a duration estimated between 100 and 300 days21,22. Other notable events include the 2019 MHWs in the Chukchi and Siberian Seas, and the 2012 Beaufort Sea MHW.Long-term changesAll Arctic MHW studies use fixed baselines spanning the 1980s to 2010s–2020 s to estimate long-term changes associated with Arctic warming. Arctic MHWs have considerably increased in frequency, duration, and intensity since the 1980s19,20,21, with an abrupt shift around mid-2000s19,20,45. Intensity trends are largest in shelf and first-year ice edge areas, exceeding +0.5 °C per decade over shelves21,53,54 and reaching +0.6 °C per year along ice edges19. Duration has increased more rapidly in the Arctic than anywhere else globally, with increases of +40–100 days since the 1980s62. Frequency increases in Arctic marginal seas are approximately double the global rate, with around +1 event per decade in the Barents45,51,54 and Bering Seas53. In the subsurface, intensity trends between 50 and 500 m are as large as or larger than at the surface17, although bottom trends in the Barents Sea show lower frequency increases and near-zero or negative intensity trends54.Attribution of these trends differs from the physical mechanisms triggering individual events (Fig. 1d), a distinction not always made explicit in the Arctic MHW literature. The former is addressed here, the latter in the following section. The observed changes are attributed to the superimposition of two interconnected mechanisms driven by greenhouse gas forcing22: mean ocean warming, which increases the probability of exceeding MHW thresholds, and sea-ice decline, which exposes the ocean surface to the atmosphere and amplifies both the mean warming and SST variability20,21,45. Importantly, SST variability changes play a larger role in driving Arctic MHW trends than in other ocean basins, where mean warming dominates63: in the Arctic, both contributions are roughly comparable27,64.Future projections consistently indicate continued increases in Arctic MHW frequency, duration, and intensity2,27, driven by the same mechanisms. The Arctic is projected to experience the largest intensity and frequency increases globally26,64. Projected intensification by 2100 reaches approximately 1.5 times the global mean under a fixed baseline, and up to 7 times when isolating variability-driven changes26. Duration projections remain uncertain, with models disagreeing on the sign of change26,64.Which mechanisms drive Arctic marine heatwaves?We review the drivers and feedback that regulate the onset and decay of individual MHWs in the contemporary Arctic.Atmospheric driversAnomalous atmospheric heating is the primary driver of Arctic surface MHWs61, as elsewhere in the global ocean, but its expression is strongly modulated by sea-ice presence, as discussed below. Surface MHWs are most prevalent in marginal and shelf seas where summer sea-ice retreat exposes open water to solar radiation, allowing larger SST variations22,56,61 and anomalies than at lower-latitudes20,26.Sustained anomalous southerly winds are consistently identified as an initiating mechanism21,45,55,56, driving sea-ice retreat and warm air advection65, though the associated pressure patterns differ across studies. In the Barents Sea, some identify a high-pressure anomaly over the eastern Barents Sea56 or the eastern North Atlantic45. In the Bering Sea, MHWs correlate with Alaskan air temperatures but not with the Arctic Oscillation53. Heatwaves over neighboring land masses have also been observed to co-occur with MHWs in Arctic marginal seas, as during the 2020 Siberian heatwave61,66. Remote teleconnections have further been suggested5, with Indian Ocean SST variability for Eurasian Arctic MHWs and with the Southern Annular Mode for Canadian Arctic MHWs, though the mechanistic links remain to be established.Influence of sea-ice coverSea ice exerts a central control on Arctic MHWs by regulating air–sea heat fluxes. During summer, peak solar input around July means that early sea-ice melt allows more solar radiation to reach the ocean surface, amplifying warming through the ice–albedo feedback61,67. For instance, Arctic MHWs in 2007 and 2020 absorbed nearly twice the usual summer solar energy due to extremely low ice cover22. Regional sea-ice patterns further modulate MHW potential: landfast ice over the Siberian shelf insulates surface waters, suppressing local MHW formation but limiting ice export, thereby expanding open water and MHW likelihood offshore21.Importantly, melting strengthens the salinity-driven stratification of the upper Arctic Ocean, producing a shallower mixed layer that confines heat to a thinner fresh surface layer68, where less heat input suffices to sustain MHWs. Model simulations estimate that this effect lengthens and intensifies surface MHWs by a striking 20% on average61. The resulting prolonged events in turn accelerate sea-ice loss, reinforcing the positive feedback between surface warming, melt, and stratification19 (box A in Fig. 3). Together, these processes explain the observed prevalence of intense Arctic MHWs in marginal seas and first-year ice areas. There, wind-driven ice-edge displacement, meltwater stratification, and shallow water columns limiting heat dissipation, all favor MHW development.Fig. 3: Drivers of Arctic MHWs.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imageConceptual summary of mechanisms directly evidenced to drive Arctic MHWs (bold text), indirectly documented processes (plain text), and unknown effects (italics marked with a star) with potentially important contributions. White arrows indicate the relationship between each mechanism. Mechanisms specific to the Arctic polar system are framed in gray.Cloud effectsCloud cover also modulates atmospheric heat fluxes at the ocean surface, influencing both the onset and intensity of MHWs3,5,6. In non-polar oceans, MHWs are often associated with a low cloud–shortwave–SST feedback, whereby warmer SSTs reduce low cloud cover, allowing greater incoming solar (i.e., shortwave) radiation that reinforces MHWs69,70,71. In the Arctic, however, this feedback is largely unexplored. Enhanced insolation during the boreal summer does intensify surface MHWs22. Yet higher Arctic SSTs and expanded open water are associated with increased cloudiness72,73, in contrast to non-polar oceans. Although greater cloud cover reduces incoming solar radiation, this cooling can be offset by reduced surface albedo from Arctic sea-ice loss74 and enhanced longwave radiation75,76. We therefore hypothesize a possible cloud–longwave–SST feedback dominated by enhanced cloudiness rather than increased insolation. Indeed, recent increases in summer and autumn surface MHWs coincide with modest cloud-cover rises20. The relative importance of shortwave-driven and longwave-driven cloud–SST feedbacks is therefore uncertain. Additionally, Arctic cloud radiative impacts are strongly seasonal77 and phase-dependent78,79, warming the surface in winter via longwave radiation and slightly cooling it in summer by shading. This adds further complexity to the net effect of Arctic clouds on MHWs, which thus remains an open question (box B in Fig. 3).Vertical ocean heat fluxesDownward ocean heat fluxes account for over two-fifths of Arctic surface MHW dissipation61, driven by vertical mixing or entrainment from spatiotemporal variations in mixed-layer depth61,80. Beyond surface MHW decay, we postulate these fluxes can also drive MHWs at depth. This mechanism was directly demonstrated at lower latitudes16,18 and is plausible in the Arctic, where warm anomalies isolated below the surface by salinity-driven restratification have been documented81. We further propose two pathways which could trigger re-emerging MHW conditions at the surface82, based on processes documented for Arctic warm anomalies but not yet examined in a MHW context. First, Arctic mixed layer deepening in fall or winter can re-entrain isolated heat towards the surface81. Second, warm summer anomalies can delay freezing and promote wind-driven openings in thin sea-ice, facilitating upward fluxes of subsurface heat83,84,85.Beyond downward fluxes, warm water masses (mainly Atlantic-origin water) are found in the Arctic subsurface, from which heat is episodically transferred upward into the overlying water column (box C in Fig. 3). Such upward ocean heat fluxes contribute to about one-fifth of surface MHW onsets61. We expect that they can similarly generate subsurface MHWs, though their specific contribution remains unquantified. Several processes drive these upward fluxes. Wind-driven mixing and upwelling can bring subsurface heat toward the surface, even under sea ice68,86, and can supply heat from boundary currents onto shallower shelves86,87. Where warm subsurface waters lie close to the surface, such as north of Svalbard or in the Bering Strait, this can occasionally inhibit sea-ice formation88,89. Winter thermohaline convection, triggered by surface cooling and brine rejection during ice formation, can bring subsurface heat upward in regions influenced by Atlantic Water31, a process further enhanced by poleward winds promoting sea-ice export84,85. Its direct link to MHWs is, however, not yet established. The presence of subsurface heat, with no equivalent at lower latitudes, makes these processes particularly consequential in the Arctic and warrants dedicated investigation.Additional mechanisms can generate upward heat fluxes, although whether they produce anomalies of sufficient magnitude and persistence to drive MHWs remains unknown and is less likely. Planetary waves or Ekman pumping can vertically displace the thermocline, producing warm anomalies through upward heaving (in contrast to downward heaving typical of non-polar oceans18), but their effectiveness in the strongly salinity-stratified Arctic is uncertain. Tidal mixing over continental shelves, particularly in Atlantic Water inflow regions, can also generate turbulent heat fluxes90.Horizontal advectionLateral ocean heat advection is particularly important near Arctic gateways and shelf breaks, where Atlantic or Pacific heat anomalies are transported into the Arctic basin32. In these regions, advection has been found to dominate over surface heat fluxes in triggering both surface54,61 and subsurface MHWs51,54. Advection also propagates heat anomalies into the ocean interior91,92, including through mesoscale activity and warm-core eddies93,94, which could plausibly produce transient MHW conditions. The contribution of eddies for both MHW onset and decay remains unquantified despite increasingly energetic Arctic Ocean conditions30. In contrast, modeling studies suggest that riverine heat input is largely negligible beyond estuaries21.Together, the drivers described hereinabove shape the intensity, duration, and spatial patterns of Arctic MHWs. How the relative contributions of these drivers will evolve in a warming Arctic remains an open question.What are the impacts of Arctic marine heatwaves?Arctic MHWs drive changes across the physical, cryospheric, and atmospheric components of the Arctic system, with consequences for ecosystems and biogeochemical cycles.Oceanic properties and ice meltArctic MHWs alter ocean properties. During surface MHWs, the positive feedback between high SSTs and enhanced ice melt19 strengthens upper-ocean salinity stratification33,61, limiting vertical exchanges with deeper waters. Conversely, we hypothesize that subsurface MHWs could erode this Arctic halocline stratification: warm anomalies isolated below the mixed layer, from anomalous advection or thermocline heaving18, can thermally weaken the pycnocline, facilitating vertical mixing. Winter convection or upwelling events transfer heat to the surface, possibly generating upper-ocean MHWs by mixing or entraining subsurface heat into colder surface water, and thereby homogenizing the water column33,84.As described hereinabove, amplified surface warming during MHWs also enhances sea-ice melt and exposes darker ocean surfaces, reinforcing the ice–albedo feedback and further enhancing melt22,55,61,67 (box I in Fig. 4). The resulting sea-ice retreat in turn enhances warm Atlantic inflow95, possibly further promoting MHWs. Anomalous upward ocean heat fluxes from upwelling88,89, mixing68, convection31,96, or subsurface heat re-entrainment97, similarly accelerate sea-ice melt or delay freezing, implying the same impacts for MHWs generated by these mechanisms.Fig. 4: Impacts of Arctic MHWs.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imageConceptual summary of identified impacts of Arctic MHWs (bold text), suspected impacts through documented responses to anomalous warming (plain text), and effects remaining unclear (italics marked with a star) on components of the Arctic system plausibly sensitive to MHWs. Arctic-specific impacts are framed in gray. DMS: Dimethyl sulfide, CO2: carbon dioxide.Warm Atlantic Water intrusions into Greenland fjords are a primary driver of glacier mass loss98,99,100. An important yet unestablished possibility is that MHW-associated ocean warming could similarly intensify glacier melting and enhance the likelihood of calving events, with potential implications for tipping points in the Greenland ice sheet101. In contrast, Arctic subsea permafrost thaws over decades to centuries via gradual ocean warming and salt diffusion102,103. Short-term MHWs are unlikely to have a noticeable impact, although shallow coastal sediments could experience temporary warming or slightly accelerated thaw.Clouds and weatherFinally, Arctic MHWs can influence atmospheric properties by modifying surface heat fluxes and cloud formation, although this has not been directly quantified. MHWs could reduce low clouds via the shortwave–SST feedback20,22, or enhance mid- to high-level clouds through increased atmospheric moisture and the longwave–SST feedback proposed above. Similar to lower-latitude oceans, prolonged warm and ice-free conditions could also support light penetration, favoring primary production, and increase emissions of dimethyl sulfide, a precursor to cloud-condensation nuclei104,105. Elevated dimethyl sulfide can in turn alter cloud radiative properties106, forming a plausible but undocumented physical–biogeochemical feedback for Arctic MHWs.The broader impacts of Arctic MHWs on neighboring continental weather are largely unknown. At lower latitudes, MHWs have been shown to amplify extreme coastal precipitation107, but polar regions have not been examined. The proposed link between Arctic warming and mid-latitude weather patterns through jet stream changes108 is debated109, and the influence of Arctic MHWs remains uncharacterized.Ecological responsesWhile increased temperatures generally support increased phytoplankton growth110, highly variable light and nutrient conditions make MHW impacts on Arctic primary production strongly context-dependent, and unpredictable from stable warming responses alone52. At sub-Arctic32 or lower latitudes7, MHWs enhance productivity under nutrient-replete conditions but reduce chlorophyll concentrations when nutrients are scarce12,104. In the Arctic, ice-sparse conditions associated with MHWs can alleviate light limitation, supporting earlier blooms and extended growth seasons111,112, though at the expense of ice-associated algae important for the food web105,113. MHW-enhanced stratification limits mixing33,39, while delayed freeze-up favors wind mixing and nutrient replenishment114,115. Arctic MHW effects on primary production thus vary and remain insufficiently investigated.Beyond productivity, anomalous warming shifts Arctic phyto- and zooplankton community composition116,117, generally favoring smaller plankton types under nutrient-depleted conditions34,110, reducing food quality for higher levels of the food web. Such shifts are documented in sub-Arctic regions34 but largely unexamined in the Arctic. We postulate that, as at lower latitudes, such plankton changes could further influence Arctic heat budgets via ocean color changes and solar radiation absorption118.Reduced food availability and quality, combined with the high thermal sensitivity of polar species and increased metabolism under warm conditions, can trigger cascading effects across the Arctic food web (box II in Fig. 4)33,34. Many Arctic species are vulnerable to MHWs while subpolar species mainly benefit from them, but these impacts are complex, vary strongly across species34,119, and remain insufficiently assessed. Available studies report reduced lipid levels in polar cods120, harmful algal blooms, and seabird mortality121 linked to anomalously warm Arctic Ocean temperatures. We expect that subsurface MHWs could similarly affect benthic communities across Arctic shelves, which sustain high biomass and productivity in Atlantic and Pacific inflow regions35,122,123. On Arctic inflow shelves, long-term warming and pulses of warm Atlantic or Pacific waters have driven northward expansions of lower-latitude species and community shifts across trophic levels, from plankton124,125 to benthic communities126,127,128,129,130. Similar MHW-driven expansions are plausible, but remain unestablished. Finally, whether MHWs affect Arctic benthic communities—including benthic-pelagic coupling131,132,133—remains unclear despite their variable but often limited thermal tolerance134.Air–sea CO2 fluxesMHWs can also affect oceanic carbon uptake by modulating air–sea fluxes of carbon dioxide (CO2), yet no existing study has examined this in polar regions13,135. The net effect of Arctic MHWs on air–sea CO2 fluxes is thus unknown, and several counteracting mechanisms make it challenging to anticipate (box III in Fig. 4). The surface Arctic Ocean generally has lower CO2 partial pressures than the atmosphere136, so MHW-induced sea-ice retreat may enhance CO2 uptake. Meltwater input further lowers the oceanic partial pressure of CO2 through the dissolution of ikaite—a carbonate mineral formed in sea ice36,137,138. Conversely, warming decreases CO2 solubility, potentially offsetting these effects; for example, 4 °C anomalies could shift the Arctic region from a carbon sink to a source36. Biological responses to MHWs, including changes in primary productivity and organic matter remineralization, could further modulate CO2 uptake.Potential for compound eventsMHW impacts can be exacerbated when co-occurring with other environmental extremes139. The Arctic is particularly vulnerable to compound MHW-acidification events: regional ocean acidification is already among the most intense globally42,43,44, and anomalous warming can temporarily elevate high-acidity event risks by increasing hydrogen ion concentrations and the partial pressure of CO2 in seawater140. Global modeling studies project a pronounced future rise in compound MHW–acidity extremes in the Arctic140,141. At lower latitudes, thermally-driven acidity increases can be offset by CO2 outgassing and reduced upward dissolved inorganic carbon supply under stronger stratification140. In the Arctic, however, strong salinity-driven stratification and carbon-undersaturated surface waters136 limit this compensation, and alkalinity from sea-ice meltwater offers only limited buffering137. By contrast, warming alone increases the saturation state of the calcium carbonate mineral aragonite142: this makes waters less corrosive to aragonite and potentially reduces stress on calcifying organisms such as shelled pteropods and mollusks143.In the Arctic, extreme subsurface deoxygenation has also been observed144, but no study has directly examined compound MHW-low oxygen events. At lower latitudes, MHWs have been linked to deoxygenation139,141 due to reduced oxygen solubility in warmer waters. Such compound events are plausible, but likely of lesser magnitude given the generally high oxygen saturation of the Arctic Ocean.Methodological recommendationsThe application of standard MHW methods to the Arctic raises challenges that are largely absent at lower latitudes. We discuss these and propose methodological adaptation where most needed.Detection thresholds and baselinesThe rapidly changing Arctic conditions make MHW statistics especially sensitive to the choice of detection threshold and baseline. We recommend combining fixed and shifting baselines46: the former quantifies the contribution of long-term warming and suits slow-adapting ecosystems, while the latter accounts for both mean and variability changes, allowing MHW detection relative to the fully updated Arctic state. If detrending is employed, daily detrending should be used over yearly detrending, which introduces seasonal artefacts in the Arctic due to the strong seasonality of warming trends.Yet several Arctic-specific complications remain. First, the pace of Arctic change implies that even 30-year shifting windows aggregate substantially different climatic conditions, impacting threshold height. Second, the contrast in variability regimes between ice-covered and ice-free periods complicates conventional threshold definition, particularly during melt and freeze-up seasons. As the open-water season lengthens, these transition periods shift in timing, inducing long-term changes in subseasonal variance: whether and how these propagate into threshold estimates through the standard 30-day smoothing window4 remains unstudied in polar regions. We encourage future research to acknowledge these limitations when interpreting results.Beyond these statistical considerations, threshold choice also carries biological implications. Ecological responses to Arctic MHWs vary strongly across species34,119 and remain insufficiently documented. Importantly, gap duration and warm-cold alternation further modulate Arctic phytoplankton responses52. We recommend that future studies explicitly state the biological rationale for their chosen percentile threshold, gap tolerance, and minimum duration as a standard reporting practice, and that studies targeting fast-responding organisms test definitions with shorter event windows.Metrics for characterizationWe identify three additional metric gaps specific to the Arctic. First, the onset rate (also called abruptness) is overlooked but particularly relevant in the Arctic for benthic and ice-dwelling species with limited mobility combined with narrow thermal ranges33. Second, the MHW activity index145, which integrates intensity, duration, and areal extent, can complement the cumulative intensity alone and is less sensitive to Arctic SST variance than MHW categories57. Third, current MHW detection tracks only liquid ocean temperature, and is therefore blind to latent heat consumed by sea-ice melt—a term with no equivalent at lower latitudes. An energetic perspective treating the ice-ocean system as a whole could provide a relevant new angle for Arctic MHW research, complementing temperature-based approaches.Spatial characterization is also incomplete: the vertical structure of Arctic MHWs remains almost entirely uncharacterized. Only two studies have examined subsurface MHW conditions in the Arctic51,54 (Fig. 1a), despite evidence that subsurface events are as intense and more prolonged than surface ones17. The framework of Malan et al.18 offers the most complete vertical structure classification and is well-suited for Arctic application, pending one adjustment. The approach is designed for temperature-stratified oceans and uses the mixed layer depth and thermocline as reference levels. In the salinity-stratified Arctic, the halocline is a more physically meaningful reference. Following Malan (personal communication, 2026), we recommend incorporating salinity or density profiles alongside temperature in the clustering algorithm to reflect the full thermohaline structure of the Arctic water column. Horizontally, Lagrangian and object-based detection approaches15 would provide more coherent characterizations and better capture interactions between warm water and sea ice at the ice edge.Data sources and uncertaintiesArctic MHW research has relied primarily on satellite products and model simulations (Fig. 1c), with in-situ data so far not employed. Importantly, all satellite-based studies use OISST v2.1 (Gou et al.33 being the exception), despite a 2016 discontinuity from a revision of the sea-ice-cover to SST conversion146. This reliance introduces uncertainty: MHW trends can diverge or even reverse sign across satellite products in ice-covered regions50. We therefore recommend diversifying data sources, testing alternative satellite SST products, ocean reanalyses, and model large ensembles64 or wind-nudged experiments147 to separate sampling uncertainty from systematic biases. Under-ice observing capacity should also be expanded through ice-tethered profilers, polar Argo floats, and gliders, with in-situ detection methods adapted for sparse sampling148. Finally, MHW sampling should be integrated into large-scale programs such as Tara Drifting Station missions149 and event-based strategies150.Summary and research outlookIn this Perspective, we assessed established mechanisms of Arctic MHWs, proposed complementary ones supported by broader oceanographic literature, and outlined remaining knowledge gaps. We contend that Arctic MHWs are inherently different from MHWs elsewhere, yet this difference has been partially overlooked. Sea ice is not solely a regional feature: it creates distinct MHW processes, governing heat exchange, stratification, and temperature variability in ways absent from non-polar oceans. Beyond sea ice-ocean feedbacks, we propose that the Arctic subsurface heat and the processes supplying this heat upwards represent an overlooked driver class with no equivalent at lower latitudes.Our synthesis shows that Arctic marginal seas are consistently identified as surface MHW hotspots, while subsurface MHWs remain almost entirely uncharacterized despite likely being as intense and more prolonged than surface events. We assert that the future of Arctic MHW research lies at the subsurface: the field may currently be missing a large part of the Arctic MHW signal. Furthermore, the contribution of variability changes to Arctic MHW trends is larger than in other oceans, meaning that standard baseline choices and attribution methods can misrepresent MHW detection and bias interpretation of results. We have proposed concrete steps to address these challenges and facilitate future Arctic MHW studies, and we identified outstanding research priorities summarized in Table 1.Table 1 Summary of the proposed scientific priorities for future Arctic MHW research, building on the knowledge gaps identified in this PerspectiveFull size tableAlongside MHWs, cold-adapted Arctic ecosystems face the concurrent stressors of acidification and sea-ice loss, but their responses to these extremes are so far insufficiently documented. Finally, several key effects remain uncertain due to competing processes: the net influence of clouds and eddies on Arctic MHWs, and net Arctic MHW impacts on air–sea CO₂ fluxes and primary production.Superimposed on the already exceptional long-term warming trends in the Arctic, MHWs can accelerate the fundamental changes unfolding in this region. Advancing our understanding of Arctic MHWs is essential for projecting ecological and climatic responses in the Arctic and their consequences for the broader Earth system.MethodsSystematic literature synthesisWe identified relevant peer-reviewed literature published up to March 2026 through searches with Scopus, Google Scholar, and the AI tool A2i Asta. Searches combined the keywords “marine heatwave” with at least one of the following: “Arctic,” “Barents Sea,” “Kara Sea,” “Siberian Sea,” “Chukchi Sea,” “Bering Sea,” “Beaufort Sea,” or “Greenland Sea.” Studies were included in the corpus based on the following criteria: (i) the study must present data north of 60°N; (ii) studies masking ice-covered regions were excluded; (iii) for global studies, at least one of the geographic keywords must appear in the main text (excluding references and methods sections); (iv) global studies were further excluded if they provided no specific information on Arctic MHWs.Aggregation of Arctic MHW statisticsOnly Arctic-focused studies were retained for the aggregation of MHW statistics. Global studies were excluded on the grounds that the MHW metrics in individual Arctic sectors cannot be reliably extracted from the provided figures or tables. For each retained study, we extract the range of reported values for each metric within each Arctic sub-region, except where a single event-averaged or regionally-averaged value is reported. Where no data are available for a given metric, the entry is recorded as NaN. Metrics are aggregated at the following resolutions:
                  
                    Mean intensity (°C): bins of 0.1 °C.
                  
                  
                    Mean duration (days): bins of 5 days.
                  
                  
                    Mean frequency (events per year): bins of 0.5 events per year.
                  
                A total of 15 studies were included in the aggregation of MHW statistics (Supplementary Table 1). Their distribution across the seven Arctic sectors is shown in Fig. 1, and the resulting aggregation of these statistics in Fig. 2.

Data availability
              
              All data discussed in this review are from previously published studies, all cited in the reference list. The Arctic surface marine heatwave statistics compiled across published studies and presented in Fig. 2 and Supplementary Fig. 3 are available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20412942 (Athanase et al.151). The model data used to generate the Supplementary Figs. 1 and 2 can be retrieved at https://doi.org/10.14428/DVN/JSDC8Q (Barthélemy et al.152).

items.title: Tartarughe marine, al via la  stagione con i primi nidi siciliani
items.description:
Con il mese di giugno è partita ufficialmente la stagione riproduttiva di Caretta caretta sulle coste della Penisola. Volontari WWF in azione
The post
items.body: In evidenza WWF Italia: 60 anni per la natura In evidenza Il Panda Siamo Noi In evidenza L'orso bruno marsicano In evidenza Adotta una specie Network WWF in azione sulle spiagge Con il mese di giugno è partita ufficialmente la stagione riproduttiva di Caretta caretta sulle coste della Penisola. A farla da padrone, manco a dirlo, le spiagge siciliane. Dopo il primo nido a Lampedusa, due nidi sono stati deposti il 2 giugno nel siracusano, prima a Marzamemi e poi a Pachino (scoperti grazie ai monitoraggi dei volontari Silvio Ariazzi e Lisa Pancaldi il primo, Ilaria Sperelli Pizzocri e Caludio Guidi, il secondo, e poi verificati e censiti dalla biologa WWF Oleana Prato). Nido anche a Mazara del Vallo (TP), segnalato da un cittadino alla Capitaneria di Porto: coinvolti i referenti locali del WWF per la messa in sicurezza del prezioso carico di uova. Quarto nido a Punta Braccetto (Ragusa). Trovato da Salvatore Liali, che ha contattato la Capitaneria di Pozzallo. Sul posto l’operatrice Laura Paderni. Anche nell’Oasi WWF di Torre Salsa (Siculiana, Agrigento) sono state rilevate tracce di risalita, ma per ora nessuna deposizione. Nido Caretta caretta a Pachino, Ragusa (2025) Volontari e cittadini a fianco degli esperti Le squadre WWF, insieme a volontari e cittadini formati, sono attive fin dalle prime ore del giorno per individuare tracce di risalita delle tartarughe sulla spiaggia, segnalare e mettere in sicurezza i nidi, coordinarsi con le autorità competenti, sensibilizzare bagnanti e operatori turistici. Un’attenzione particolare sarà rivolta anche alla gestione dei lidi e alle buone pratiche per ridurre il disturbo antropico. Le attività di monitoraggio dallo scorso anno si inseriscono nel quadro del progetto europeo LIFE ADAPTS, cofinanziato dall’Unione Europea, che ha come obiettivo quello di sviluppare strategie di adattamento al cambiamento climatico per tutelare habitat e specie vulnerabili come la foca monaca e le tartarughe marine del Mediterraneo. Il progetto utilizza tecnologie avanzate (eDNA, droni, fototrappole, telemetria satellitare), coinvolge le comunità locali e promuove soluzioni replicabili per la protezione delle spiagge di nidificazione. Numeri in costante aumento Tra il 2020 e il 2025 gli Operatori del WWF hanno registrato ben 1.055 nidi tra Sicilia, Calabria, Basilicata e Puglia, con una crescita particolarmente marcata negli ultimi 3 anni. Di questi, ben 738 in Sicilia, il 70% di tutti quelli deposti in Italia, poi 223 in Calabria e 94 nell’arco ionico (Golfo di Taranto: Puglia, Basilicata e Nord Calabria). La scorsa stagione riproduttiva il WWF ha contato ben 338 nidi di tartaruga marina, il valore più alto mai registrato.Con l’avvio della stagione di nidificazione delle tartarughe marine, in Calabria il WWF torna a lanciare l’allarme sul continuo utilizzo di mezzi meccanici sulle spiagge per le operazioni di pulizia (in particolare nella provincia di Vibo). Una pratica, purtroppo diffusa anche nelle altre regioni, che può compromettere la sopravvivenza dei nidi di Caretta caretta e mettere a rischio altre specie protette, come il fratino, oltre alla preziosa e delicata vegetazione dunale. LIFE ADAPTS Adattamento al cambiamento climatico per la tutela di tartarughe marine e foche monache del Mediterraneo Scopri di più Tartaruga Marina Scopri di più

items.title: “Un Mare di Amici” approda alla Fortezza e Darsena vecchia a Livorno
items.description: Il 5 giugno si celebra la Giornata Mondiale dell’Ambiente, istituita dalle Nazioni Unite nel 1972 per promuovere la consapevolezza e stimolare azioni

                                                “Un Mare di Amici” approda alla Fortezza e Darsena vecchia a Livorno

“Un Mare di Amici” approda alla Fortezza e Darsena vecchia a Livorno

Temi ambientali:
                                                                                                            
                                            Acque marino costiere
                                        
                                                                                                                                                
                                            Biodiversità
                                        
                                                                                                                                                Cetacei
                                                                                                                                                Posidonia
                                                                                                                                                Tartarughe

Oltre 700 studenti livornesi protagonisti dell’evento conclusivo del progetto di Educazione ambientale e alla sostenibilità, svoltosi il 29 maggio

Il 5 giugno si celebra la Giornata Mondiale dell’Ambiente, istituita dalle Nazioni Unite nel 1972 per promuovere la consapevolezza e stimolare azioni globali a tutela dell’ambiente. Tra le iniziative che accompagnano le celebrazioni di oggi segnaliamo Pulifondali e Pulispiagge dedicata alla raccolta di rifiuti spiaggiati e sommersi, comprese reti fantasma e plastiche, che si svolge anche in cinque città toscane, tra cui Livorno, con il patrocinio di ARPAT.
In questo contesto si inserisce perfettamente il progetto “Un Mare di Amici”, che si è concluso venerdì 29 maggio nello splendido scenario della Fortezza Vecchia e della Darsena Vecchia del Porto Mediceo di Livorno. L’evento si è svolto, per la prima volta, in concomitanza con Navi di Maggio, iniziativa promossa dall’Autorità di Sistema Portuale del Mar Tirreno Settentrionale in occasione della Giornata Europea del Mare.
Protagonisti della giornata sono stati oltre 700 studenti e studentesse provenienti da dieci istituti scolastici della provincia di Livorno. Tra loro anche gli alunni coinvolti durante l’anno scolastico nelle attività educative realizzate da ARPAT, Capitaneria di Porto, ASL Toscana Nord Ovest e Società Nazionale Salvamento (guarda la galleria di immagini della giornata).
Il progetto, promosso con la convinta adesione dell’Ufficio Scolastico Provinciale di Livorno, è finalizzato a sviluppare percorsi di educazione alla salute e alla tutela dell’ambiente, con particolare attenzione al mare e alle coste, secondo l’approccio “One Health”.
Per gli studenti l’iniziativa ha rappresentato un’importante occasione di apprendimento e scoperta, permettendo loro di conoscere da vicino luoghi, attività e professioni legate al mondo marino, approfondire temi tecnico-scientifici e condividere buone pratiche per la tutela e la fruizione sostenibile del mare e delle coste.
Gli studenti e le studentesse hanno visitato gli stand dei promotori dell’iniziativa, insieme a quelli degli altri enti impegnati nella difesa del mare e dell’ambiente e nella ricerca scientifica applicata al mare e al clima, oltre che delle associazioni di volontariato. L’esperienza è stata arricchita dalla possibilità di visitare le motovedette e i mezzi navali delle diverse autorità e  enti che operano in mare e di assistere anche a dimostrazioni di soccorso e salvamento in mare.
Molti ragazzi sono saliti a bordo del nuovo catamarano Polaris di ARPAT dove sono stati accolti dal personale dell’Agenzia. I biologi hanno illustrato le principali strumentazioni utilizzate per le attività di monitoraggio e tutela dell’ambiente marino, tra cui quelle impiegate per il controllo della qualità delle acque, dei sedimenti, delle microplastiche e della biodiversità marina, nonché per il monitoraggio di habitat particolarmente sensibili, come le praterie di Posidonia oceanica.

Presso lo stand allestito alla Fortezza Vecchia, i biologi di ARPAT hanno coinvolto gli studenti in attività laboratoriali e giochi educativi dedicati alla scoperta della ricca biodiversità marina del Santuario Pelagos. Attraverso esperienze interattive, i ragazzi hanno potuto conoscere da vicino alcune delle specie che popolano questo importante tratto di mare, tra cui balenottere, delfini, squali e la tartaruga marina Caretta caretta, che negli ultimi anni ha scelto sempre più spesso le spiagge toscane per la nidificazione.
L’iniziativa è stata anche l’occasione per divulgare il progetto Life Turtlenest, di cui ARPAT è partner, finalizzato a rafforzare la tutela della nidificazione della  Caretta caretta attraverso attività di monitoraggio, ricerca e sensibilizzazione. Un ruolo centrale è affidato anche ai cittadini, chiamati a collaborare nella segnalazione e nel monitoraggio dei nidi lungo la costa toscana.
Nello stand situato sulla banchina della Darsena vecchia, condiviso con la Capitaneria di Porto, i biologi di ARPAT hanno invece mostrato le tecniche di campionamento e analisi delle acque marine, spiegando come vengono effettuati i controlli che contribuiscono a garantire la qualità delle acque di balneazione. Tra le attività che hanno suscitato maggiore interesse, l’osservazione allo stereomicroscopio di macroinvertebrati di acqua dolce e una piccola stella marina raccolta proprio nella Darsena vecchia. I ragazzi hanno seguito con curiosità e meraviglia i movimenti delle sue cinque sottili braccia, trasformando un momento di osservazione scientifica in un’esperienza capace di avvicinarli concretamente alla vita che popola i nostri mari.
Tra gli studenti presenti all’evento, anche i ragazzi e le ragazze della classe 3F della scuola secondaria di primo grado dell’Istituto Comprensivo “Micheli-Bolognesi” (plesso ex Pirelli) che, a seguito degli incontri in classe con il personale ARPAT per il progetto Un mare di amici, hanno sviluppato un percorso creativo dedicato al mare e alla sua tutela. Il risultato è una raccolta di suggestive installazioni artistiche realizzate con materiali di recupero, accompagnate da disegni e poster che raccontano la straordinaria biodiversità del Santuario Pelagos e le principali minacce che mettono a rischio molte specie marine in particolare vertebrati marini e tartarughe: collisioni con le navi, catture accidentali nelle reti da pesca e inquinamento da microplastiche.
A conclusione dell’evento alcuni studenti hanno avuto l’opportunità di assistere, al rilascio in mare di due esemplari di tartaruga Caretta caretta, “Nella” e “Vera”, grazie alle unità messe a disposizione da Piloti, Ormeggiatori, Vigili del Fuoco e dalla Lega Navale Italiana. Le due tartarughe, recuperate nei mesi scorsi in difficoltà e curate dall’Acquario di Livorno, sono state liberate dopo un percorso di riabilitazione: la prima era stata trovata a galleggiare nelle acque del porticciolo del Chioma tra numerosi rifiuti, la seconda soccorsa al largo di Livorno da una motovedetta della Guardia di Finanza, avvinghiata da due galleggianti che le impedivano di nuotare ed immergersi. Curate nell’apposito Centro di recupero e riabilitazione sono state portate in mare aperto dal personale dell’Acquario a bordo di due motovedette della Guardia di Finanza e della Guardia Costiera.
Le attività svolte durante la manifestazione hanno mostrato come la conoscenza dell’ambiente non possa essere separata dalla consapevolezza delle relazioni che legano gli esseri viventi, gli ecosistemi e le azioni umane.
Un messaggio particolarmente significativo se si considera che proprio nel giorno dell’evento, il 29 maggio, ci ha lasciato il filosofo Edgar Morin, la cui famiglia materna, ebrea sefardita, era originaria proprio di Livorno prima di trasferirsi a Parigi. Lo ricordiamo oggi perché Morin ha dedicato una vita intera a difendere la complessità del reale, senza ridurla o semplificarla. Siamo debitori e debitrici di uno sguardo sull’educazione che non separa ciò che è unito, che non riduce ciò che è vivo. Pietra miliare della sua riflessione è la teoria della complessità – la necessità di una nuova conoscenza interdisciplinare che superi la separazione dei saperi e che sia capace di educare ad un pensiero della complessità.
La parola ‘complesso’, amava ricordare, significa ‘ciò che è intessuto assieme’: un approccio indispensabile per indagare innanzitutto l’essenza dell’umanità, senza più tracciare frontiere artificiali fra ciò che è naturale e ciò che è culturale. La testimonianza di Edgar Morin non si spegne con il tempo, ma si fa compito: manteniamo viva la sua lezione, insegnando alle nuove generazioni l’arte di  “abitare la complessità del mondo senza smarrire la poesia della vita”.
Il video con le interviste ai partecipanti alla giornata

items.title: Giornata mondiale dell’ambiente, parte l’inchiesta WWF
items.description:
Il WWF lancia un questionario per capire come la crisi climatica sta trasformando la nostra quotidianità
The post Giornata mondiale dell’ambient
items.body: In evidenza WWF Italia: 60 anni per la natura In evidenza Il Panda Siamo Noi In evidenza L'orso bruno marsicano In evidenza Adotta una specie “La crisi climatica è già qui, cosa sta cambiando per te?” L’obiettivo è dare la possibilità ai cittadini di raccontare l’impatto degli eventi estremi sulla propria vita e verificare il grado di preparazione sui territoriWWF Italia Link diretto al questionario >In occasione della Giornata Mondiale dell’Ambiente del 5 giugno, il WWF Italia lancia l’indagine nazionale “La crisi climatica è già qui: cosa sta cambiando per te?”. L’iniziativa, realizzata con il sostegno del Movimento Laudato Si’ e altre realtà civiche, si avvale della partnership scientifica del CMCC (Centro Euro-Mediterraneo sui Cambiamenti Climatici) e della collaborazione di Green&Blue come principale media partner. La raccolta dei dati durerà fino a metà settembre. Le domande riguardano soprattutto l’impatto degli eventi estremi, dalle piogge estreme alle ondate di calore, ma non tralasciano anche eventi più prolungati, come la siccità, tutti fenomeni ormai sempre più diffusi anche in Italia, con l’obiettivo di valutare la percezione da parte della popolazione degli effetti della crisi climatica sulla loro quotidianità, sullo stile di vita e sulle abitudini personali. L’indagine verificherà inoltre le misure attive già prese dalla popolazione e dalle comunità per affrontare concretamente il rischio climatico nella propria realtà e sul proprio territorio oltre ai sistemi di tutela e protezione da parte di istituzioni e assicurazioni per supportare, proteggere e aiutare i cittadini nella gestione e nel recupero a seguito di queste emergenze. I risultati della raccolta dati, validati scientificamente dal CMCC, saranno pubblicati a fine settembre 2026. L'inchiesta del WWF Vai alla pagina e partecipa all'indagine sulla crisi climatica Partecipa Europa colpita da ondata di calore L’iniziativa parte in un momento cruciale: il mese di maggio 2026 ha infatti registrato forti ondate di calore in diverse regioni del Pianeta, una tuttora in corso in molte aree dell’Europa occidentale, tra cui l’Italia: si sono registrate temperature molto superiori alla media stagionale, arrivando agli incredibili 35°C del Regno Unito (a Heathrow e Kew Gardens). Un nuovo studio di attribuzione di ClimaMeter pubblicato in questi giorni ha concluso che:• Le alte temperature che hanno causato l’ondata di caldo dell’Europa occidentale del maggio 2026 sono da attribuire al cambiamento climatico causato dall’uomo, mentre la variabilità climatica naturale ha probabilmente svolto un ruolo minore.• Le condizioni di caldo più estreme, aggravate dal cambiamento climatico, hanno messo a rischio circa 124 milioni di persone e un’attività economica pari a 5.885 miliardi di dollari.• Questo evento è stato associato a condizioni meteorologiche rare che, in passato, si verificavano principalmente in autunno, ma che ora stanno emergendo anche in primavera. Nella tarda primavera, tuttavia, possono favorire lo sviluppo di una cupola di calore, amplificando condizioni già calde. Crisi climatica va veloce La crisi climatica procede a ritmi molto più rapidi rispetto alla reale capacità di risposta e protezione della cittadinanza e dei territori. Il mondo naturale come lo conosciamo è sconvolto dagli impatti del cambiamento climatico, la sua distruzione rischia di amplificarne gli effetti: eppure, con il suo ripristino, costituisce un alleato decisivo. I dati* relativi all’Italia dell’ultimo periodo evidenziano la gravità della situazione: Mesi totali con eventi estremi: saliti da 30-31 (anni ’80-’90) a 69 (2021-2025).Ondate di calore: mesi interessati cresciuti dal 13% (2001-2010) al 38% (2011-2020), fino al 53% nel 2021-2025 (6,5 mesi l’anno).Temperature massime estreme: 7 mesi sopra la norma nell’anno idrologico 2024-2025, con picco massimo a giugno 2025.Freddo estremo: mesi con gelo scesi dal 7% al 2%; 11 mesi su 12 privi di anomalie fredde nell’ultimo anno.Precipitazioni estreme: mesi interessati quadruplicati, dal 3% al 12% tra i primi due decenni del secolo.Incendi boschivi: frequenza mensile salita fino al 14% nel periodo 2021-2025.Venti di forte intensità: incidenza salita fino al 10% dei mesi. Nel 2025 ben 17 ondate di calore In media in Italia nel 2025 sono state rilevate 17 ondate di calore (+5,5 rispetto al 2010), 14 picchi di caldo estremo (che arrivano a 18 nelle città del Centro, quasi dieci eventi in più rispetto al 2010) e circa 80 notti tropicali (23 in più rispetto a quindici anni fa).Tra il 1980 e il 2023, i danni complessivi provocati in Italia da eventi climatici estremi hanno superato i 135 miliardi di euro, posizionando il nostro Paese al secondo posto in Europa per perdite economiche complessive legate al clima. A ciò si aggiunga, come evidenziato nel report del WWF “Tackling the Insurance Protection Gap: leveraging climate and nature to increase resilience”, che il divario di protezione assicurativa italiano è tra i più alti in Europa. Appena il 20% delle perdite da eventi estremi è coperto da polizze, mentre il restante 80% grava interamente sulle finanze di famiglie, imprese e finanze pubbliche, minando la stabilità economica generale e rendendo le comunità sempre meno assicurabili. Poiché gli assicuratori reagiscono aumentando i premi, limitando la copertura o ritirandosi dalle aree ad alto rischio, sempre più persone e imprese rimangono esposte. A confermare la vulnerabilità sociale interviene anche l’ultimo rapporto dell’Agenzia Europea per l’Ambiente (AEA) e di Eurofound, intitolato “Overheated and underprepared: Europeans′ experiences of living with climate change”. L’indagine mette in luce una forte asimmetria tra la percezione del rischio e la reale preparazione: ben l’80% dei cittadini europei (4 su 5) dichiara di aver già subito un forte disagio legato al clima negli ultimi 5 anni, ma nonostante i riscontri diretti dei danni, oltre il 22% delle famiglie europee non ha adottato alcuna misura di protezione o adattamento nella propria abitazione, palesando una grave impreparazione strutturale. L’indagine del WWF L’indagine del WWF nasce dunque dall’esigenza di capire in che modo la popolazione italiana stia rispondendo a queste sfide e come stia affrontando l’impatto della crisi nella vita di ogni giorno. L’obiettivo è raccogliere dati preziosi per comprendere la distanza tra i rischi climatici effettivi e le reali difese di cui le persone dispongono, analizzando sia il livello di preparazione individuale sia le tutele fornite dalle istituzioni locali. Grazie alla Citizen science, la cittadinanza diventa la vera protagonista del monitoraggio nazionale, segnalando in prima persona: Frequenza e gravità dei danni e dei disagi subiti a livello locale.Livello di tutela percepito rispetto alle politiche climatiche attuate dalle autorità.Specifiche politiche di adattamento e mitigazione realmente applicate sul proprio territorio.Conoscenza ed effettivo utilizzo di strumenti assicurativi (pubblici e privati) contro i danni climatici. Questa mappatura dal basso permetterà di far emergere le priorità d’azione necessarie per colmare il divario di protezione attuale e rafforzare la sicurezza delle comunità di fronte alla crisi climatica. Per partecipare attivamente alla compilazione del questionario, mettendo a disposizione la propria esperienza compila il modulo disponibile qui > *Fonte: Report “Come sta l’Italia? Analisi degli eventi meteorologici fuori dall’ordinario 2024/2025”, basato sull’European Extreme Events Climate Index (E3CI) Senti già la crisi climatica? A partire dal 5 Giugno, Giornata Mondiale dell’Ambiente, il WWF Italia, con il sostegno del Movimento Laudato Si’ ed altre realtà civiche promuove un’indagine partecipata per analizzare l’impatto concreto e già in atto della crisi climatica sulla vita quotidiana della popolazione, il… Scopri di più

items.title: Sversamento di gasolio in un corso d’acqua a Montale: concluse le prime verifiche
items.description: Personale del Dipartimento ARPAT di Pistoia è intervenuto lo scorso 1° giugno, su attivazione della Polizia Locale di Montale, dopo il rinvenimento di
items.body:
Home
                    
                            
                            
                                                                    
                        Ufficio stampa
                    
                            
                            
                                                                    Comunicato stampa
                            
                            
                                                Sversamento di gasolio in un corso d’acqua a Montale: concluse le prime verifiche

Sversamento di gasolio in un corso d’acqua a Montale: concluse le prime verifiche

Temi ambientali:
                                                                                                            Emergenze ambientali
                                                                                                
                        
                                                                                                                
                                    Tag:
                                                                            Acque superficiali,                                                                            Sversamento                                                                    
                                                    
                                    
            
            
            
                
                                            Pubblicato il:
                        
                            5 Giugno 2026

Condividi

Condividi

                    Condividi su X

                    Condividi su Whatsapp

Proseguono gli accertamenti

Personale del Dipartimento ARPAT di Pistoia è intervenuto lo scorso 1° giugno, su attivazione della Polizia Locale di Montale, dopo il rinvenimento di tracce di idrocarburi in un corso d’acqua situato nell’area di via Alfieri, affluente del torrente Bure.
Durante il sopralluogo, i tecnici ARPAT e il personale della Polizia Locale hanno effettuato un’approfondita attività ispettiva lungo diversi tratti del corso d’acqua e delle aree limitrofe, risalendo progressivamente verso monte nel tentativo di individuare l’origine dello sversamento.
Le verifiche hanno confermato la presenza di gasolio nelle acque e in alcuni punti del reticolo idraulico interessato. Tuttavia, nonostante gli accertamenti eseguiti in varie zone del territorio comunale, non è stato possibile individuare con certezza il punto di origine dello sversamento.
Al momento del sopralluogo, il fenomeno risultava già in fase di esaurimento. La presenza di vegetazione all’interno dell’alveo ha inoltre favorito la formazione di piccoli ristagni nei quali il gasolio si è concentrato, rendendo più evidente e persistente la sua presenza.
Le intense precipitazioni che hanno interessato il territorio pistoiese nel pomeriggio del 1° giugno potrebbero aver contribuito al dilavamento di depositi di sostanze presenti in aree a monte, ipotesi che sarà oggetto di ulteriori approfondimenti.
Nel corso delle operazioni è stata richiesta l’attivazione delle misure di contenimento mediante l’installazione di barriere assorbenti per limitare la propagazione del materiale inquinante lungo il corso d’acqua.
L’Agenzia ha infine richiesto al Comune di Montale ulteriori verifiche cartografiche sul percorso di una fossa campestre presente nell’area di via Croce Rossa, al fine di ricostruire con maggiore precisione il reticolo idraulico e individuare eventuali ulteriori punti ispezionabili utili alle indagini tutt’ora in corso.

items.title: Broadly quantifying SSP elements for 188 countries to 2150 in International Futures
items.body:
Download PDF

Article
    
        
            Open access

Published: 05 June 2026

Broadly quantifying SSP elements for 188 countries to 2150 in International Futures
                    Jonathan D. Moyer 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-6947-06631, Barry B. Hughes 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-0007-33871, Mohammod Irfan 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0009-0001-0011-40701, José Solórzano1, Caleb L. Petry 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-1269-229X1, Yutang Xiong 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-2365-27601, Taylor Hanna 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0009-0002-1864-88441, Collin J. Meisel1, Deva Sahadevan 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0009-0007-1566-09671 & …Ethan Sullivan 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0009-0000-9046-85011 Show authors

Nature Communications

(2026) Cite this article
                    
                    
                    
                            We are providing an unedited version of this manuscript to give early access to its
                            findings. Before final publication, the manuscript will undergo further editing. Please note
                            there may be errors present which affect the content, and all legal disclaimers apply.

Subjects
        
            Projection and prediction

AbstractWe extend the quantification of the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs) narratives by projecting 188 socioeconomic indicators for 188 countries to 2150 using the International Futures model. The variables span demographics, conflict, economics, education, health, infrastructure, and governance using a fully integrated model. We address existing limitations related to a) misalignment between quantification and scenario narratives; b) inconsistencies driven by use of unconnected models; c) the omission of indicators; and d) absence of projections into the 22nd century. Here we show projections highly correlated with existing forecasts that also lead to lower economic growth because they are driven by broader factors including conflict, governance, infrastructure, and human development, which in turn leads to lower resource demand. We also introduce government spending measures by sector that can be used to connect the SSPs to broader theoretical understandings of future patterns of mitigation and adaptation in the face of climate change.

items.title: Un passo verso il “nucleare sostenibile” in Italia. Ma la strada è ancora lunga
items.description: Primo ok alle legge delega, ma i primi reattori modulari di piccole dimensioni arriveranno tra 10 anni. E per la fusione se ne parla dopo il 2040.
items.body:
Cos'è il nucleare sostenibile lanciato dal governo italiano?

L'Italia farà la sua parte per triplicare l'energia nucleare globale entro il 2050

L’Italia farà la sua parte per triplicare l’energia nucleare globale entro il 2050
		
		
			Ufficiale l’impegno al rafforzamento, nel mix energetico, della quota prodotta con i reattori di terza e quarta generazione. Ecco come ci siamo arrivati. 
		
		
			 Energia
			20 marzo 2026
			
				di Simone Santi

Energia sostenibile, il convegno del gruppo Marazzato traccia la rotta verso il futuro
		
		
			Venticinque esperti si confrontano su comunità energetiche, nucleare di nuova generazione, biocarburanti e termovalorizzazione dei rifiuti: “Serve coraggio e una coscienza collettiva”.
		
		
			 Energia
			12 giugno 2025
			
				di Redazione

Cos’è il nucleare sostenibile lanciato dal governo italiano?
		
		
			Il governo vara il ddl che segna l’inizio del ritorno al nucleare, di nuova generazione, per contribuire alla decarbonizzazione. Quali pro e quali contro?
		
		
			 Energia
			12 marzo 2025
			
				di Simone Santi e Maurizio Bongioanni

Anche Google punta sul nucleare per “accendere” l’intelligenza artificiale
			
			
				Dopo Microsoft, anche Google stringe un accordo energetico con il settore nucleare. Intanto, il fabbisogno dell’ia continua a crescere, crescere, crescere.

Energia

17 ottobre 2024

Perché il Kazakistan ha scelto di puntare sul nucleare proprio adesso
			
			
				Il Kazakistan, in epoca sovietica martoriato dai testi atomici, ha scelto con un referendum di affidarsi al nucleare. Non convince però l’esito del voto

Esteri

14 ottobre 2024

L’Italia accelera sul nucleare, ma il deposito per le scorie si allontana
			
			
				Si punta arrivare al net zero del 2050 con almeno l’11 per cento di energia prodotta dai nuovi reattori, tutto fermo però sulla gestione delle scorie.

Energia

10 ottobre 2024

Three Mile Island riaccenderà i reattori per alimentare l’intelligenza artificiale di Microsoft
			
			
				L’impennata dei consumi energetici dovuti allo sviluppo dell’intelligenza artificiale fa ripartire persino la centrale di Three Mile Island, famosa per il disastro nucleare peggiore degli Stati Uniti.

Tecnologia

29 settembre 2024

Il nucleare non fa bene al clima, secondo Hervé Kempf
			
			
				Nel suo ultimo libro, edito in Italia da Einaudi, il giornalista francese fornisce il suo parere sul perché il nucleare non è un buon investimento per il clima.

Energia

26 aprile 2024

Una batteria atomica dalla durata decennale: l’invenzione arriva dalla Cina
			
			
				L’azienda cinese Betavolt annuncia la creazione di una batteria atomica con energia nucleare sicura e a lunga durata per dispositivi medici e non solo.

Tecnologia
